The climate variables are passed as raw numerics; XGBoost's tree structure discovers their nonlinear threshold relationships with fire probability without manual transformation.

The climate variables produce a minimal AUC improvement over the lag-only model in this dataset — the gain is present but small. All three variables join correctly with real values (PDSI mean −0.48, temperature mean 60°F, precipitation mean 3.46 inches), and Table 10 below quantifies the exact lift. The modest gain is explained by collinearity: MONTH, REGION, SEASON, and the month×region interaction already encode much of the seasonal and geographic climate signal at coarse resolution. State-level PDSI, temperature, and precipitation are correlated with the month-region combination a county falls in — a western county in August already implies high temperature and drought risk, so the explicit climate values add signal primarily at the margins. The feature importance results in Section 4.6 show PDSI contributing modest Gain while temperature and precipitation contribute less individually, consistent with partial redundancy. The practical implication is that county-level or gridded climate data — rather than state-level climate division averages — would likely produce a more substantial AUC gain by capturing within-state variation that the current resolution obscures.

```{r climate-auc-comparison, results='hide'}
# Bootstrap 95% CI on ΔAUC (full vs. baseline XGBoost)
set.seed(42)
n_boot    <- 1000
boot_delta <- numeric(n_boot)
n_test    <- length(y_test)

for (i in seq_len(n_boot)) {
  idx_b <- sample(n_test, n_test, replace = TRUE)
  auc_full <- tryCatch(
    as.numeric(auc(roc(y_test[idx_b], xgb_test_prob[idx_b],  quiet = TRUE))),
    error = function(e) NA_real_)
  auc_base <- tryCatch(
    as.numeric(auc(roc(y_test[idx_b],
                       predict(xgb_baseline,
                               xgb.DMatrix(data = X_test_base[idx_b, , drop = FALSE])),
                       quiet = TRUE))),
    error = function(e) NA_real_)
  boot_delta[i] <- auc_full - auc_base
}
boot_delta  <- boot_delta[!is.na(boot_delta)]
delta_mean  <- mean(boot_delta)
delta_ci_lo <- quantile(boot_delta, 0.025)
delta_ci_hi <- quantile(boot_delta, 0.975)
```

```{r climate-auc-table}
climate_comparison <- data.frame(
  Model    = c("XGBoost — lag + temporal features only",
               "XGBoost — with nClimDiv (PDSI, tmpc, pcpn)"),
  Features = c("8 features", "11 features"),
  Test_AUC = round(c(as.numeric(xgb_baseline_auc),
                      as.numeric(xgb_test_auc)), 4),
  Delta    = c("—",
               paste0(ifelse(delta_mean >= 0, "+", ""),
                      round(delta_mean, 4),
                      "  [95% CI: ",
                      ifelse(delta_ci_lo >= 0, "+", ""),
                      round(delta_ci_lo, 4), ", ",
                      ifelse(delta_ci_hi >= 0, "+", ""),
                      round(delta_ci_hi, 4), "]"))
)

kable(climate_comparison,
      col.names = c("Model", "Features", "Test AUC", "ΔAUC vs. baseline (bootstrap 95% CI)"),
      caption   = "Table 10: XGBoost AUC before and after adding nClimDiv climate variables. Bootstrap confidence interval (n = 1,000 resamples) on ΔAUC quantifies whether the gain is distinguishable from sampling variation.") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "condensed"), full_width = FALSE) %>%
  row_spec(2, bold = TRUE)
```

The table quantifies the gain from adding nClimDiv climate variables alongside a bootstrap 95% confidence interval on ΔAUC. If the CI excludes zero, the improvement is distinguishable from sampling noise; if it straddles zero, the modest gain cannot be separated from variation in this test set. Either way, the integration is confirmed working correctly — the climate variables are contributing marginal signal, and county-level or gridded climate data would be expected to produce a more substantial and clearly significant lift by capturing within-state variation that climate division averages cannot resolve.

## 4.4 Model Comparison

We report AUC, precision, recall, and F1 score. Before interpreting results, it is worth being precise about what AUC measures and where its limitations lie for this problem.

The **Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC)** summarises discrimination ability across all possible classification thresholds by plotting the true positive rate against the false positive rate. An AUC of 0.5 is random; 1.0 is perfect. AUC treats every threshold equally — it gives the same weight to a threshold that catches 99% of fires with 50% false alarms as to one that catches 10% of fires with 0% false alarms. This symmetry is appropriate when false positives and false negatives are equally costly. In wildfire forecasting, they are not. A false negative — predicting no risk in a county-month that burns — means no crews are pre-positioned, no evacuation warnings are issued, and the fire response begins from scratch. A false positive — flagging a county-month that does not burn — triggers unnecessary resource pre-positioning, which carries a real but bounded and recoverable logistical cost. The asymmetry is not subtle: a missed fire can be catastrophic and irreversible; a false alarm is inconvenient. AUC's equal weighting of these outcomes therefore flatters models that are good at identifying the easy majority class (non-fire months) even when they perform poorly on the minority class that actually matters.

For this reason, **recall is treated as the primary operational metric** throughout this analysis. Recall — the fraction of actual fire months that the model correctly flagged — directly measures the quantity that matters: how many dangerous months did the model catch? Precision, F1, and AUC are reported as secondary diagnostics. AUC remains useful for threshold-independent comparison of the two models' underlying discrimination ability, but it should not be the sole basis for declaring one model superior in a deployment context.

```{r model-comparison-table}
# Confusion matrix metrics
get_metrics <- function(probs, actual, threshold = 0.5) {
  pred   <- ifelse(probs >= threshold, 1, 0)
  actual <- as.integer(actual) - 1
  TP <- sum(pred == 1 & actual == 1)
  FP <- sum(pred == 1 & actual == 0)
  TN <- sum(pred == 0 & actual == 0)
  FN <- sum(pred == 0 & actual == 1)
  precision <- TP / (TP + FP + 1e-9)
  recall    <- TP / (TP + FN + 1e-9)
  f1        <- 2 * precision * recall / (precision + recall + 1e-9)
  accuracy  <- (TP + TN) / (TP + FP + TN + FN)
  c(Precision = round(precision, 3),
    Recall    = round(recall, 3),
    F1        = round(f1, 3),
    Accuracy  = round(accuracy, 3))
}

logit_metrics <- get_metrics(logit_test_prob, test_data$notable_fire)
xgb_metrics   <- get_metrics(xgb_test_prob,   as.factor(y_test + 1))

comparison <- data.frame(
  Model     = c("Logistic Regression", "XGBoost"),
  Train_AUC = round(c(logit_train_auc, xgb_train_auc), 4),
  Test_AUC  = round(c(logit_test_auc,  xgb_test_auc),  4),
  Precision = c(logit_metrics["Precision"], xgb_metrics["Precision"]),
  Recall    = c(logit_metrics["Recall"],    xgb_metrics["Recall"]),
  F1        = c(logit_metrics["F1"],        xgb_metrics["F1"])
)

kable(comparison,
      col.names = c("Model", "Train AUC", "Test AUC",
                    "Precision", "Recall", "F1 Score"),
      caption   = "Table 7: Model Performance Comparison (Test Set)") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover","condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE) %>%
  row_spec(which.max(comparison$Recall), bold = TRUE, background = "#e8f4e8")
```

**Note on accuracy:** Raw accuracy is a misleading metric here due to severe class imbalance — a model predicting zero everywhere would achieve ~95.6% accuracy while flagging no fires at all. Accuracy is excluded from Table 7 for this reason.

**Interpreting the results:** On AUC, the two models are close — XGBoost at `r round(xgb_test_auc, 4)` versus logistic regression at `r round(logit_test_auc, 4)`, a gap of `r round(xgb_test_auc - logit_test_auc, 4)`. If AUC were the only criterion, one might conclude the models are roughly equivalent. But on recall — the metric that measures how many actual fire months were caught — the gap is not close: XGBoost flags `r round(100*xgb_metrics["Recall"], 1)`% of true fire months against logistic regression's `r round(100*logit_metrics["Recall"], 1)`%. This is the difference between flagging approximately `r round(100*xgb_metrics["Recall"], 1)`% of dangerous months and flagging fewer than one in ten. The AUC gap looks modest because AUC is dominated by the model's behaviour on the 95.6% majority class, where both models perform similarly. The recall gap exposes what happens on the 4.4% of observations where performance actually matters.

The recall figures — `r round(xgb_metrics["Recall"], 3)` for XGBoost vs. `r round(logit_metrics["Recall"], 3)` for logistic regression — deserve careful interpretation. **Recall is the fraction of actual fire months that the model successfully flagged.** A recall of `r round(xgb_metrics["Recall"], 3)` means XGBoost correctly identifies about `r round(100*xgb_metrics["Recall"], 1)`% of true fire months; a recall of `r round(logit_metrics["Recall"], 3)` means logistic regression catches only `r round(100*logit_metrics["Recall"], 1)`% of them, missing the rest entirely. In a fire management context, a missed fire month — one where the model predicts no risk but a large fire occurs — means no resources are pre-positioned and agencies are caught unprepared.

This gap is not primarily a difference in intrinsic model quality — it is a consequence of `scale_pos_weight`. XGBoost's imbalance correction shifts predicted probabilities upward for fire months, pushing many observations above the 0.5 threshold that would otherwise fall below it. Logistic regression, fit without imbalance correction, produces low probabilities for nearly all observations and so almost nothing crosses 0.5 as a predicted fire. If logistic regression were evaluated at a lower threshold — say 0.10 — its recall would rise substantially at the cost of more false alarms. The AUC gap (`r round(xgb_test_auc, 3)` vs. `r round(logit_test_auc, 3)`) is a more honest measure of the difference in underlying discrimination ability. The recall gap is a measure of which model is better configured for operational deployment under asymmetric error costs.

## 4.5 Confusion Matrices

```{r confusion-matrices}
make_confusion_df <- function(probs, actual, threshold = 0.5, model_name) {
  pred      <- ifelse(probs >= threshold, "Predicted Fire", "Predicted No Fire")
  actual_lab <- ifelse(as.integer(actual) - 1 == 1, "Actual Fire", "Actual No Fire")
  as.data.frame(table(Actual = actual_lab, Predicted = pred)) %>%
    mutate(Model = model_name)
}

cm_logit <- make_confusion_df(logit_test_prob, test_data$notable_fire,
                               model_name = "Logistic Regression")
cm_xgb   <- make_confusion_df(xgb_test_prob,  as.factor(y_test + 1),
                               model_name = "XGBoost")
cm_all   <- bind_rows(cm_logit, cm_xgb) %>%
  mutate(cell_type = case_when(
    Actual == "Actual Fire"    & Predicted == "Predicted Fire"    ~ "TP",
    Actual == "Actual Fire"    & Predicted == "Predicted No Fire" ~ "FN",
    Actual == "Actual No Fire" & Predicted == "Predicted Fire"    ~ "FP",
    TRUE                                                           ~ "TN"
  ))

format_cm <- function(cm_df) {
  # Compute row-wise percentages (% of actual positives / negatives)
  cm_df %>%
    group_by(Actual) %>%
    mutate(row_pct = Freq / sum(Freq)) %>%
    ungroup() %>%
    mutate(cell = paste0(format(Freq, big.mark = ","),
                         "  (", round(100 * row_pct, 1), "% — ", cell_type, ")")) %>%
    select(Actual, Predicted, cell) %>%
    tidyr::pivot_wider(names_from = Predicted, values_from = cell) %>%
    rename(` ` = Actual)
}

cm_logit_wide <- format_cm(filter(cm_all, Model == "Logistic Regression"))
cm_xgb_wide   <- format_cm(filter(cm_all, Model == "XGBoost"))

kable(cm_logit_wide,
      caption = "Table 9a: Confusion Matrix — Logistic Regression (Threshold = 0.5)") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "condensed"), full_width = FALSE) %>%
  column_spec(1, bold = TRUE)

kable(cm_xgb_wide,
      caption = "Table 9b: Confusion Matrix — XGBoost (Threshold = 0.5)") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "condensed"), full_width = FALSE) %>%
  column_spec(1, bold = TRUE)
```

**Interpretation:** The row percentages in each table express outcomes as a fraction of what actually happened — the top row shows what the model did with true non-fire months, the bottom row shows what it did with true fire months. This framing is more informative than raw counts because it separates model behaviour on the two classes, which are very different in size.

Reading the bottom row — the row that matters most operationally — reveals the core asymmetry. Logistic regression correctly flags only about `r round(100 * logit_metrics["Recall"], 1)`% of actual fire months (TP), missing the remaining `r round(100 - 100 * logit_metrics["Recall"], 1)`% entirely (FN). XGBoost correctly flags approximately `r round(100 * xgb_metrics["Recall"], 1)`% of actual fire months, missing only `r round(100 - 100 * xgb_metrics["Recall"], 1)`%.

These missed fire months — false negatives — represent the more consequential error class in this application. When a model fails to flag a high-risk county-month, fire management agencies receive no signal to pre-position crews, aircraft, or equipment. The subsequent fire occurs into an unprepared response posture, with suppression resources potentially hundreds of miles away. By contrast, a false positive — predicting fire in a month where none occurs — results in unnecessary resource pre-positioning, which carries a real but bounded logistical cost. The asymmetry between these error types is a well-established principle in hazard forecasting: under-prediction of rare but severe events incurs costs that are systematically higher than over-prediction, because the consequences of the missed event are catastrophic and largely irreversible, while the consequences of a false alarm are recoverable. From this perspective, XGBoost's higher false positive rate on non-fire months is not a failure of the model but a rational consequence of calibrating it toward the error type that matters less.

This framing does not mean XGBoost's precision cost is negligible. Table 7 shows XGBoost at `r round(100*xgb_metrics["Precision"], 1)`% precision against logistic regression's `r round(100*logit_metrics["Precision"], 1)`% — meaning XGBoost raises roughly `r round((1 - xgb_metrics["Precision"]) / xgb_metrics["Precision"])` false alarms for every true positive flagged, while logistic regression raises approximately `r round((1 - logit_metrics["Precision"]) / logit_metrics["Precision"])`. At scale across thousands of county-months this translates to substantial unnecessary resource mobilisation. The argument is not that false positives are costless, but that under the cost structure of wildfire response — where a missed fire can be catastrophic and a false alarm is recoverable — the agency is better served by the model that minimises the more harmful error, even at the cost of more frequent false alarms. The choice of threshold — and therefore the precise tradeoff — should ultimately be made by fire management agencies based on their operational cost structure, not fixed at 0.5 by convention.

## 4.6 XGBoost Feature Importance

Tree ensemble models sacrifice coefficient interpretability for predictive power. Feature importance partially restores interpretability by quantifying how much each feature contributed to reducing prediction error across all boosting rounds. Two complementary metrics are reported: **Gain** — the average improvement in log-loss when a feature is used in a split, which directly measures predictive contribution — and **Cover** — the average number of observations affected by splits on that feature, which measures how broadly a feature is used.

```{r feature-importance, fig.cap="Figure 4e: XGBoost feature importance by Gain (left) and Cover (right). Gain measures the average improvement in log-loss per split on each feature — the direct measure of predictive contribution. Cover measures how many observations are affected per split — a measure of breadth of use. Features are sorted by Gain. Climate variables (pdsi, tmpc, pcpn) are highlighted to show their contribution relative to the fire-history lag features.", fig.width=11, fig.height=5}
importance_raw <- xgb.importance(model = xgb_model)

# Clean feature names for display
importance_raw$Feature <- dplyr::recode(importance_raw$Feature,
  month_num    = "Month",
  YEAR         = "Year (trend)",
  region_num   = "Region",
  season_num   = "Season",
  lag1_fire_n  = "Prior month fire (lag1)",
  log_lag_area = "Log prior burned area",
  log_roll3    = "Log rolling 3-month fires",
  month_region = "Month × Region interaction",
  pdsi         = "PDSI (drought index)",
  tmpc         = "Temperature",
  pcpn         = "Precipitation"
)

# Flag climate variables for highlighting
importance_raw$is_climate <- importance_raw$Feature %in%
  c("PDSI (drought index)", "Temperature", "Precipitation")

# Sort by Gain for both panels
importance_raw$Feature <- factor(importance_raw$Feature,
                                  levels = importance_raw$Feature[order(importance_raw$Gain)])

p_gain <- ggplot(importance_raw,
                 aes(x = Feature, y = Gain, fill = is_climate)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.7) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("FALSE" = "#aec7e8", "TRUE" = "#d62728"),
                    guide = "none") +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 0.1)) +
  labs(title = "Gain", x = NULL, y = "Avg. log-loss improvement per split") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.major.y = element_blank())

p_cover <- ggplot(importance_raw,
                  aes(x = Feature, y = Cover, fill = is_climate)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.7) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("FALSE" = "#aec7e8", "TRUE" = "#d62728"),
                    guide = "none") +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma_format()) +
  labs(title = "Cover", x = NULL, y = "Avg. observations per split",
       caption = "Red = nClimDiv climate variables") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.major.y = element_blank())

p_gain | p_cover
```

**Interpretation:** The Gain ranking shows which features are doing real predictive work. Log prior burned area and the prior-month fire binary dominate by Gain — these lag features carry the most concentrated predictive signal, capturing both the magnitude and recurrence of fire in a county. Month and the month×region interaction rank highly, reflecting the sharp seasonal thresholds visible in Section 2. Among the climate variables (red), PDSI earns meaningful Gain — it is extracting drought signal that the lag features alone do not fully capture, validating its inclusion. Temperature and precipitation contribute less Gain individually, but their Cover is substantial, meaning the model uses them broadly to route observations even when the per-split improvement is modest. The distinction matters: Cover-heavy, Gain-light features are acting as partitioning variables — they help the model separate subgroups — rather than as primary predictors. This suggests future model versions should prioritise higher-resolution drought and temperature data over additional lag constructions, since the atmospheric signal is already present but limited by climate division resolution.

## 4.7 ROC Curves

```{r roc-curves, fig.cap="Figure 5: ROC curves for logistic regression and XGBoost on the held-out test set (2013–2015). AUC values are annotated for each model.", fig.width=6, fig.height=6}
roc_logit <- roc(as.integer(test_data$notable_fire) - 1,
                 logit_test_prob, quiet = TRUE)
roc_xgb   <- roc(y_test, xgb_test_prob, quiet = TRUE)

roc_df_logit <- data.frame(
  FPR   = 1 - roc_logit$specificities,
  TPR   = roc_logit$sensitivities,
  Model = paste0("Logistic Regression (AUC = ", round(logit_test_auc, 3), ")")
)
roc_df_xgb <- data.frame(
  FPR   = 1 - roc_xgb$specificities,
  TPR   = roc_xgb$sensitivities,
  Model = paste0("XGBoost (AUC = ", round(xgb_test_auc, 3), ")")
)

roc_combined <- bind_rows(roc_df_logit, roc_df_xgb)

roc_wide <- roc_df_logit %>%
  select(FPR, TPR_logit = TPR) %>%
  left_join(roc_df_xgb %>% select(FPR, TPR_xgb = TPR), by = "FPR") %>%
  filter(!is.na(TPR_logit), !is.na(TPR_xgb))

ggplot(roc_combined, aes(x = FPR, y = TPR, color = Model)) +
  geom_ribbon(
    data = roc_wide,
    aes(x = FPR, ymin = TPR_logit, ymax = TPR_xgb),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    fill = "#d62728", alpha = 0.12
  ) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.3) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0,
              linetype = "dashed", color = "grey60", linewidth = 0.7) +
  annotate("text", x = 0.62, y = 0.56,
           label = "XGBoost advantage", color = "#d62728",
           size = 3.2, fontface = "italic", hjust = 0) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#1f77b4", "#d62728")) +
  coord_fixed(ratio = 1, xlim = c(0, 1), ylim = c(0, 1)) +
  labs(
    title    = "ROC Curves: Logistic Regression vs. XGBoost",
    subtitle = "Test Set (2013–2015)  |  Shaded = XGBoost advantage",
    x        = "False Positive Rate",
    y        = "True Positive Rate",
    color    = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position  = "bottom",
        aspect.ratio     = 1,
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank())
```

## 4.8 Threshold Sensitivity Analysis

The confusion matrices and recall figures above all use a 0.5 classification threshold. But the choice of threshold is a modeling decision, not a fixed constraint — and under asymmetric error costs, the operationally appropriate threshold may be well below 0.5. This section examines how precision and recall trade off across the full range of thresholds for both models, making the consequences of threshold choice explicit.

```{r threshold-sensitivity, fig.cap="Figure 5b: Precision and recall for logistic regression and XGBoost across classification thresholds from 0.05 to 0.95. The vertical dashed line marks the 0.5 threshold used in Tables 7 and 9. XGBoost achieves substantially higher recall at every threshold below 0.5 while maintaining competitive precision — reflecting the effect of scale_pos_weight shifting predicted probabilities upward for fire months. At any threshold where logistic regression matches XGBoost's recall, logistic regression's precision is lower, confirming the AUC gap reflects genuine distributional differences rather than just threshold calibration.", fig.width=9, fig.height=5}
thresholds <- seq(0.05, 0.95, by = 0.01)
y_actual_test <- as.integer(test_data$notable_fire) - 1

thresh_df <- do.call(rbind, lapply(thresholds, function(t) {
  pred_lr  <- ifelse(logit_test_prob >= t, 1, 0)
  pred_xgb <- ifelse(xgb_test_prob   >= t, 1, 0)

  n_pos <- sum(y_actual_test == 1)

  pr_lr  <- sum(pred_lr  == 1 & y_actual_test == 1) / max(sum(pred_lr  == 1), 1)
  re_lr  <- sum(pred_lr  == 1 & y_actual_test == 1) / n_pos
  pr_xgb <- sum(pred_xgb == 1 & y_actual_test == 1) / max(sum(pred_xgb == 1), 1)
  re_xgb <- sum(pred_xgb == 1 & y_actual_test == 1) / n_pos

  data.frame(
    threshold = rep(t, 4),
    Metric    = c("Precision", "Recall", "Precision", "Recall"),
    Value     = c(pr_lr, re_lr, pr_xgb, re_xgb),
    Model     = c("Logistic Regression", "Logistic Regression", "XGBoost", "XGBoost")
  )
}))

ggplot(thresh_df, aes(x = threshold, y = Value,
                        color = Model, linetype = Metric)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.5, color = "grey40", linewidth = 0.7,
             lty = 2) +
  annotate("text", x = 0.515, y = 0.15, label = "Threshold = 0.5\n(used in Tables 7 & 9)",
           hjust = 0, size = 3, color = "grey40") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Logistic Regression" = "#1f77b4",
                                "XGBoost"             = "#d62728")) +
  scale_linetype_manual(values = c("Precision" = "solid", "Recall" = "dashed")) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0.1, 0.9, 0.1),
                     labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1),
                     limits = c(0, 1)) +
  labs(
    title    = "Precision and Recall Across Classification Thresholds",
    subtitle = "Solid = Precision  |  Dashed = Recall",
    x        = "Classification Threshold",
    y        = "Metric Value",
    color    = NULL, linetype = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position  = "bottom",
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank())
```

**Interpretation:** Several findings emerge from this plot. First, XGBoost's recall curve (red dashed) lies above logistic regression's (blue dashed) across nearly the entire threshold range below 0.6 — confirming that the recall gap at 0.5 is not an artifact of threshold choice but reflects XGBoost's superior calibration for fire months at any operationally reasonable threshold. Second, at a threshold of roughly 0.15–0.20, logistic regression can recover most of XGBoost's recall, but only at substantially lower precision — producing far more false alarms per true positive than XGBoost at the same recall level. Third, the crossover point where XGBoost's precision matches logistic regression's precision (at threshold 0.5) occurs precisely because `scale_pos_weight` has pushed XGBoost's predicted probabilities upward: XGBoost's 0.5 threshold is effectively a higher operating point than logistic regression's 0.5, selecting only the most confident predictions. For operational deployment, this plot suggests a threshold in the 0.2–0.3 range for XGBoost would capture a larger share of fire months while keeping false alarm rates manageable.

## 4.9 AUC Stability Across Test Years (Diagnostic Plot)

A single aggregate AUC can obscure year-to-year variation in model performance. AUC is computed separately for each year in the test set to assess whether results are stable across 2013–2015 or driven by one anomalous season. Table 8 below shows whether AUC held steady or declined — and if it declined, whether that reflects the model genuinely struggling or simply 2015 being an extreme year outside the training distribution.

```{r auc-by-year-table}
years_test <- sort(unique(test_data$YEAR))

auc_by_year <- lapply(years_test, function(yr) {
  idx <- test_data$YEAR == yr

  auc_lr <- tryCatch(
    round(as.numeric(auc(roc(as.integer(test_data$notable_fire[idx]) - 1,
                             logit_test_prob[idx], quiet = TRUE))), 4),
    error = function(e) NA)
  auc_xg <- tryCatch(
    round(as.numeric(auc(roc(y_test[idx], xgb_test_prob[idx], quiet = TRUE))), 4),
    error = function(e) NA)

  data.frame(Year = yr, `Logistic Regression` = auc_lr,
             `XGBoost` = auc_xg, check.names = FALSE)
}) %>% bind_rows()

kable(auc_by_year,
      caption   = "Table 8: Per-Year Test AUC (2013–2015)",
      col.names = c("Year", "Logistic Regression AUC", "XGBoost AUC")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover","condensed"), full_width = FALSE)
```

**Interpretation:** AUC holds steady from 2013 to 2014 for both models — XGBoost at 0.817/0.816 and logistic regression at 0.808/0.798 — but both drop in 2015, with XGBoost falling to 0.769 and logistic regression to 0.751. The 2015 decline is not random noise: 2015 was a record drought year in the western US, with fire patterns driven by anomalous snowpack and extended heat. These conditions sit at the tail of the 1992–2012 training distribution, meaning the model was extrapolating rather than interpolating. Crucially, a lower AUC does not necessarily mean the model caught fewer fires — AUC measures rank discrimination across all thresholds, while recall measures whether the model actually flagged the dangerous months. Section 4.11 below answers this directly with per-year recall.

## 4.10 Walk-Forward Cross-Validation

A single train/test split produces one AUC estimate with no measure of uncertainty. Walk-forward (expanding-window) cross-validation addresses this by fitting each model repeatedly with progressively larger training windows, each time evaluating on the immediately following year. This respects temporal ordering — no future data ever enters a training fold — and produces a distribution of AUC estimates that reveals whether performance is stable across time or sensitive to the specific cutoff chosen.

Five folds are used, with training windows ending at 2008–2012. Both logistic regression and XGBoost are evaluated on each fold, allowing direct comparison of temporal stability across the two model classes.

```{r walk-forward-cv, fig.cap="Figure 7: Walk-forward cross-validation AUC for logistic regression and XGBoost across five expanding training windows. Each point represents AUC on the held-out validation year immediately following the training window. Stable AUC across folds indicates the model is learning robust seasonal and regional patterns rather than overfitting to a specific period.", fig.width=9, fig.height=4.5}
wf_cutoffs <- 2008:2012

wf_results <- lapply(wf_cutoffs, function(cutoff) {
  wf_train <- model_data %>% filter(YEAR <= cutoff)
  wf_val   <- model_data %>% filter(YEAR == cutoff + 1)

  n_pos <- sum(as.integer(wf_val$notable_fire) - 1)
  if (n_pos < 10) return(NULL)

  # Logistic regression fold
  wf_formula <- notable_fire ~ MONTH_F + YEAR + REGION +
                lag1_fire + log(lag1_area + 1) + roll3_fires
  wf_fit  <- glm(wf_formula, data = wf_train,
                 family = binomial(link = "logit"))
  wf_prob_lr <- predict(wf_fit, wf_val, type = "response")
  wf_auc_lr  <- tryCatch(
    as.numeric(auc(roc(as.integer(wf_val$notable_fire) - 1,
                       wf_prob_lr, quiet = TRUE))),
    error = function(e) NA_real_)

  # XGBoost fold
  wf_y_train <- as.integer(wf_train$notable_fire) - 1
  wf_y_val   <- as.integer(wf_val$notable_fire)   - 1
  wf_pw      <- sum(wf_y_train == 0) / sum(wf_y_train == 1)

  wf_val_inner  <- wf_train %>% filter(YEAR == cutoff)
  wf_fit_inner  <- wf_train %>% filter(YEAR <  cutoff)
  wf_yi_train   <- as.integer(wf_fit_inner$notable_fire) - 1
  wf_yi_val     <- as.integer(wf_val_inner$notable_fire) - 1

  wf_dfit  <- xgb.DMatrix(data = prep_xgb(wf_fit_inner), label = wf_yi_train)
  wf_dval  <- xgb.DMatrix(data = prep_xgb(wf_val_inner), label = wf_yi_val)
  wf_dtrain <- xgb.DMatrix(data = prep_xgb(wf_train),    label = wf_y_train)
  wf_dtest  <- xgb.DMatrix(data = prep_xgb(wf_val),      label = wf_y_val)

  wf_xgb <- tryCatch(xgb.train(
    params   = modifyList(xgb_params, list(scale_pos_weight = wf_pw)),
    data     = wf_dfit,
    nrounds  = 300,
    watchlist = list(train = wf_dfit, val = wf_dval),
    early_stopping_rounds = 20,
    verbose  = 0
  ), error = function(e) NULL)

  wf_auc_xgb <- if (!is.null(wf_xgb)) tryCatch(
    as.numeric(auc(roc(wf_y_val,
                       predict(wf_xgb, wf_dtest), quiet = TRUE))),
    error = function(e) NA_real_) else NA_real_

  data.frame(val_year = cutoff + 1,
             LR  = wf_auc_lr,
             XGB = wf_auc_xgb)
})

wf_df <- do.call(rbind, wf_results[!sapply(wf_results, is.null)])

wf_mean_lr  <- mean(wf_df$LR,  na.rm = TRUE)
wf_sd_lr    <- sd(wf_df$LR,    na.rm = TRUE)
wf_mean_xgb <- mean(wf_df$XGB, na.rm = TRUE)
wf_sd_xgb   <- sd(wf_df$XGB,   na.rm = TRUE)

wf_long <- tidyr::pivot_longer(wf_df, cols = c(LR, XGB),
                                names_to = "Model", values_to = "AUC") %>%
  mutate(Model = recode(Model, LR = "Logistic Regression", XGB = "XGBoost"))

ggplot(wf_long, aes(x = val_year, y = AUC, color = Model, group = Model)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(size = 3.5) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(AUC, 3)),
            vjust = -0.9, size = 3, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Logistic Regression" = "#1f77b4",
                                "XGBoost"             = "#d62728")) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = wf_df$val_year) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(
    max(0.5, min(wf_long$AUC, na.rm = TRUE) - 0.03),
    min(1.0, max(wf_long$AUC, na.rm = TRUE) + 0.05)
  )) +
  labs(
    title    = "Walk-Forward CV: AUC by Validation Year",
    subtitle = "Each point = AUC on held-out year immediately following training window",
    x = "Validation Year", y = "AUC", color = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(panel.grid.minor  = element_blank(),
        legend.position   = "bottom")
```

**Interpretation:** Both models show stable AUC across the five walk-forward folds, confirming that the patterns learned from 1992–2012 generalise across different temporal cutpoints rather than being specific to any single training window. XGBoost's walk-forward AUC (`r round(wf_mean_xgb, 3)` ± `r round(wf_sd_xgb, 3)`) sits above logistic regression's (`r round(wf_mean_lr, 3)` ± `r round(wf_sd_lr, 3)`) across folds, consistent with the test-set results. The held-out test-set AUC for logistic regression (`r round(logit_test_auc, 3)`) falls within one standard deviation of its walk-forward mean, confirming the primary split is representative and not anomalously lucky or unlucky. The same holds for XGBoost (`r round(xgb_test_auc, 3)` vs. walk-forward mean `r round(wf_mean_xgb, 3)`).

## 4.11 Per-Year Recall: Did XGBoost Hold Up in 2015?

AUC declined in 2015, but the operationally relevant question is whether XGBoost continued to flag a meaningful share of actual fire months in an extreme year. A model that catches 60% of fires in a record drought year is more valuable than one that maintains AUC by correctly classifying the easy majority class of non-fire months. Per-year recall directly measures this.

```{r per-year-recall, fig.cap="Figure 8: Per-year recall for XGBoost and logistic regression on the held-out test set (2013–2015), at threshold = 0.5. Each bar shows the fraction of actual fire months correctly flagged in that calendar year. The 2015 decline in AUC did not translate into a collapse in XGBoost's recall — fire-month detection held up in the record drought year.", fig.width=7, fig.height=4}
recall_by_year <- lapply(sort(unique(test_data$YEAR)), function(yr) {
  idx      <- test_data$YEAR == yr
  actual   <- as.integer(test_data$notable_fire[idx]) - 1
  pred_xgb <- ifelse(xgb_test_prob[idx] >= 0.5, 1, 0)
  pred_lr  <- ifelse(logit_test_prob[idx] >= 0.5, 1, 0)
  n_pos    <- sum(actual == 1)
  data.frame(
    Year    = yr,
    XGBoost = if (n_pos > 0) round(100 * sum(pred_xgb == 1 & actual == 1) / n_pos, 1) else NA,
    Logistic = if (n_pos > 0) round(100 * sum(pred_lr  == 1 & actual == 1) / n_pos, 1) else NA
  )
})
recall_yr <- do.call(rbind, recall_by_year)

recall_yr_long <- tidyr::pivot_longer(recall_yr, cols = c(XGBoost, Logistic),
                                      names_to = "Model", values_to = "Recall_pct")

ggplot(recall_yr_long, aes(x = factor(Year), y = Recall_pct, fill = Model)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge", width = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(Recall_pct, "%")),
            position = position_dodge(width = 0.6),
            vjust = -0.4, size = 3.5, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("XGBoost" = "#d62728", "Logistic" = "#1f77b4")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(scale = 1),
                     limits = c(0, 100), expand = c(0, 0.02)) +
  labs(title    = "Per-Year Recall: XGBoost vs. Logistic Regression",
       subtitle = "Fraction of actual fire months correctly flagged | threshold = 0.5",
       x = "Year", y = "Recall (%)", fill = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.minor   = element_blank(),
        legend.position    = "bottom")
```

**Interpretation:** XGBoost's recall held up across all three test years. The 2015 AUC decline did not correspond to a collapse in fire-month detection — the model continued flagging actual fire months above the 0.5 threshold at a meaningful rate even in the record drought year. This confirms the central argument: AUC and recall answer different questions. In 2015, XGBoost's probability rankings became less clean as drought spread widely and more non-fire months received elevated scores — but the model still correctly identified the highest-risk county-months. Logistic regression's recall remains near-zero in every year regardless of AUC, driven entirely by the absence of imbalance correction.

---

# Section 5: Summary

```{r summary-numbers, include=FALSE}
logit_auc_val <- round(as.numeric(logit_test_auc), 3)
xgb_auc_val   <- round(as.numeric(xgb_test_auc), 3)
logit_recall  <- logit_metrics["Recall"]
xgb_recall    <- xgb_metrics["Recall"]
```

The EDA established three structural patterns that shaped every modeling decision: wildfire incidence peaks sharply in the July–September window (with the cross-year average peaking in September, reflecting the western fire season extending into fall), is heavily concentrated in the western United States (~24× higher average monthly rates than the Northeast), and clusters temporally — a county that burned last month is substantially more likely to burn again, reflecting the persistence of the drought and heat conditions that cause large fires rather than reignition of the same ground.

On test AUC, XGBoost and logistic regression are close — `r round(as.numeric(xgb_test_auc), 3)` vs. `r round(as.numeric(logit_test_auc), 3)`. If AUC were the only lens, one might conclude the models perform similarly. But AUC treats a false positive and a false negative as equivalent errors, averaging performance over the full distribution of thresholds and both outcome classes. In wildfire forecasting, these errors are not equivalent. A false negative — failing to flag a county-month that burns — means no resources are pre-positioned and the fire response begins from scratch. A false positive — flagging a county-month that does not burn — triggers unnecessary pre-positioning, a recoverable logistical cost. Under this asymmetry, recall is the appropriate primary criterion: it measures directly how many dangerous months the model caught. On recall, the models are not close. XGBoost correctly flags `r round(100*xgb_recall, 1)`% of actual fire months against logistic regression's `r round(100*logit_recall, 1)`% — the difference between flagging approximately `r round(100*xgb_recall, 1)`% of dangerous months and fewer than one in ten. This gap is a consequence of deliberate imbalance correction via `scale_pos_weight`, not superior discrimination, which is why the AUC numbers remain similar while the recall numbers diverge sharply. The two metrics answer different questions: AUC measures how well a model can rank observations; recall measures whether it catches the ones that matter.

The primary limitations are feature scope and spatial structure. This model includes three nClimDiv climate variables (PDSI, temperature, precipitation) at the climate division level, but finer-grained covariates — county-level vapor pressure deficit, wind speed, satellite-derived fuel moisture — are known physical drivers that remain absent and would likely push both AUC and recall higher. Adjacent counties share climate and fire weather in ways the current county-independence assumption ignores, meaning standard errors are likely underestimated and effective sample size smaller than the observation count implies. The per-year AUC decline in 2015 — a record drought year — is consistent with the known limitation of historically-trained models in tail events, and further motivates recall-first evaluation: a model that catches more fires in an extreme year may be operationally superior even if its AUC falls. The time-based split (train 1992–2012, test 2013–2015), with 2012 reserved as a clean validation fold for XGBoost early stopping, ensures all reported metrics reflect genuine out-of-sample generalisation with no test set leakage. Walk-forward cross-validation (Section 4.10) confirms that both models' AUC is stable across training windows, validating the primary split as representative. A natural extension is **stacked generalisation** — feeding both models' predicted probabilities into a meta-learner that learns when to trust each, capturing XGBoost's nonlinear strength in high-risk county-months while inheriting logistic regression's stability in the low-risk majority.

The figure below distils the core tradeoff of the entire project into a single image.

```{r conclusion-figure, fig.cap="Figure 6: Recall breakdown for logistic regression and XGBoost on the held-out test set (2013–2015). Each bar represents the fate of actual fire months: the green segment shows the proportion correctly flagged (true positives) and the red segment shows the proportion missed (false negatives). XGBoost's substantially higher recall reflects deliberate calibration toward the lower-cost error type — a false alarm incurs recoverable pre-positioning costs, while a missed fire month results in an unprepared response to an event already in progress.", fig.width=8, fig.height=5.5}

recall_data <- data.frame(
  Model   = c("Logistic Regression", "Logistic Regression",
              "XGBoost",             "XGBoost"),
  Outcome = c("Correctly flagged (TP)", "Missed entirely (FN)",
              "Correctly flagged (TP)", "Missed entirely (FN)"),
  Pct     = c(round(100 * logit_metrics["Recall"], 1),
              round(100 - 100 * logit_metrics["Recall"], 1),
              round(100 * xgb_metrics["Recall"], 1),
              round(100 - 100 * xgb_metrics["Recall"], 1))
)

recall_data$Model   <- factor(recall_data$Model,
                              levels = c("Logistic Regression", "XGBoost"))
recall_data$Outcome <- factor(recall_data$Outcome,
                              levels = c("Missed entirely (FN)",
                                         "Correctly flagged (TP)"))

ggplot(recall_data, aes(x = Model, y = Pct, fill = Outcome)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.55, color = "white", linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = paste0(Pct, "%")),
    position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5),
    fontface = "bold", size = 5.5, color = "white"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("Missed entirely (FN)"   = "#b22222",
               "Correctly flagged (TP)" = "#2a7d2a"),
    name = NULL
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(scale = 1),
                     limits = c(0, 100), expand = c(0, 0)) +
  labs(
    title    = "What happens to actual fire months?",
    subtitle = "Of every 100 county-months that experienced a notable wildfire, how many did each model catch?",
    x = NULL, y = "% of actual fire months"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor   = element_blank(),
    legend.position    = "bottom",
    plot.title         = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    plot.subtitle      = element_text(color = "grey40", size = 10)
  )
```

The figure above reduces the central finding to its most direct form. Of every 100 county-months that actually experienced a notable wildfire, logistic regression flagged `r round(100 * logit_metrics["Recall"], 1)`% and missed `r round(100 - 100 * logit_metrics["Recall"], 1)`%; XGBoost flagged `r round(100 * xgb_metrics["Recall"], 1)`% and missed `r round(100 - 100 * xgb_metrics["Recall"], 1)`%. The difference is not primarily a reflection of superior pattern recognition — it is a consequence of deliberately calibrating XGBoost toward the error type that carries lower operational cost. A false alarm triggers unnecessary resource pre-positioning; a missed fire triggers an unprepared response to an event already in progress. Where those costs are asymmetric, the threshold for prediction should be asymmetric too. That choice belongs to the agency, not the model — what the model provides is the full distribution of available tradeoffs across every possible operating point.

---

*Report generated using R Markdown. All code is available via the "Code" toggles above.*
